quoteFirst: _ I went aboard the _Havre de Grace_
quoteLast: , and got her out of Danger into the Channel, but came to an Anchor again, by the Advice of Senior _Morell_ and the _Indian_ Pilot: I encourag’d ’em and the Men to assist me as much as possible to get her under Sail, because we were in hast to be gone, but there being little Wind, I could not make use of half the Ebb, before I was again in shole Water, and came to an Anchor, where for the want of Wind we lay for the rest of these 24 Hours.
title: A Cruising Voyage Around the World
author: Rogers, Woodes, -1732; Manwaring, G. E. (George Ernest), 1882-1939 [Annotator]

quoteLast: , his face faithfully depicting the contending passions under which he was labouring--sorrow at parting from the fleshpots of Unyanyembe--regret at parting from his Dulcinea of Tabora--to be, bereft of all enjoyment now, nothing but marches--hard, long marches--to go to the war--to be killed, perhaps, Oh!
title: How I found Livingstone
author: Stanley, Henry M. (Henry Morton), 1841-1904

quoteFirst: It was dreary work, though, waiting, for we were going along very slowly on the torpid sea, which seemed to swelter in the heat as the breeze fell; but
quoteTime: about two o'clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: the south-west wind springing up again, we once more began dancing on through the water at a quicker rate, the sampan making better progress by putting her right before wind and slacking off the sheet of our transformed sprit-sail.
title: Afloat at Last: A Sailor Boy's Log of His Life at Sea
